question: Describe, step-by-step, how the Selection Sort algorithm would sort the array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]. Include the state of the array after each major swap.
expectedPoints: Correctly identifies the first minimum (11) and swaps it with the first element (64).
Correctly identifies the next minimum (12) in the unsorted part and swaps it with the second element (25).
Correctly identifies the next minimum (22) and swaps it with the fourth element (25 from the previous state).
Correctly identifies that the last two elements are already in place, requiring no final swap.

question: Compare and contrast Bubble Sort and Selection Sort. Explain one key advantage that Selection Sort has over Bubble Sort.
expectedPoints: Both are simple comparison-based sorting algorithms with an average and worst-case time complexity of O(n²).
Bubble Sort works by repeatedly swapping adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order, 'bubbling' the largest value to the end each pass.
Selection Sort works by scanning the unsorted part for the minimum element and swapping it into the sorted position at the beginning.
A key advantage of Selection Sort is that it typically makes far fewer swaps (O(n)) than Bubble Sort (O(n²)), which can be significant if the cost of swapping is high.

question: What is the main operation that determines the time complexity of the Bubble Sort algorithm, and why is its worst-case complexity O(n²)?
expectedPoints: The main operation is the comparison (and potential swap) of adjacent elements.
In the worst case (a reverse-sorted array), every element must be compared and potentially swapped with its neighbor in each pass.
The algorithm requires n-1 passes to guarantee the array is sorted.
In the first pass, n-1 comparisons are made. In the second, n-2, and so on. This leads to the summation (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2, which is O(n²).
